figures.caption: Figure 1: Wall-pressure fluctuations at Reτ = 180, 500, and 1000 visualized in (a) inner-scaled coordinates (x+, z+) and (b) outer-scaled coordinates (x/δ, z/δ). In inner units, windows of fixed viscous-unit size capture statistically similar high-wavenumber structures across Reynolds numbers, whereas the low-wavenumber content varies systematically with Reτ in both coordinate systems. This scale separation motivates the inner-scaled patch decomposition introduced in Section 2.3.

Figure 2: Schematic of the Patched Flow Matching framework. (a) A snapshot of resolution N × N is extended to (N +2M)× (N +2M) via periodic padding, where M = (k+1)P −N and k = ⌊N/P ⌋, and decomposed into (k+1)2 non-overlapping P × P patches with a randomly chosen tiling origin. (b) The flow matching framework: a linear interpolant ϕs = sϕ1 + (1 − s)ϕ0 defines the prescribed path from noise (s = 0) to data (s = 1), and the generative ODE dϕ/ds = vθ(ϕs, s) transports samples along this path. (c) Training: the shared network vθ takes a three-channel patch tensor (ϕs, X, Z) as input and is trained to regress the conditional velocity target us = ϕ1 − ϕ0. (d) Patch selection: patches are drawn at random locations from the inner-scaled wall-pressure field (left); the corresponding normalized coordinate channels X ∈ [−1, 1] and Z ∈ [−1, 1] encode the absolute inner-scaled position of each patch within the domain (center, right).

Figure 3: Unconditional inference procedure of Patched FM. At each ODE step, a tiling origin (i, j) is drawn uniformly from {1, . . . ,M}2; the patching operator P(·, i, j) partitions the current field ϕs into (k+1)2 non-overlapping P ×P patches; each patch is processed in parallel by the shared network vθ; and the stitching operator P−1 reassembles the updated patches into the full field ϕs+ds. By randomizing the tiling origin at every step, no spatial location is persistently treated as a patch boundary, eliminating boundary artifacts in expectation.

Figure 4: Training-free conditional generation. At each ODE step, the Tweedie estimator produces a one-step prediction ϕ̂1(ϕs) of the clean field from the current noisy state ϕs. Two measurement operators provide complementary constraints: F1 extracts sparse sensor observations y1 from the interior of the current section, and F2 enforces spatial consistency y2 by matching the left boundary of the current section to the last streamwise column of the previously generated section. The gradient-based corrections v′1 and v′2 from both operators are added to the unconditional velocity vθ at each integration step, yielding posterior samples ϕ1 ∼ p1(ϕ | y).
Table 1: Domain and grid sizes of the channel flow DNS dataset. Cases 1–3 (short domain, LS x = 4πδ) are used for training; Cases 4–6 (long domain, LL x = 16πδ) are reserved exclusively for testing.

Figure 5: Unconditional generation at Reτ = 180: (a) Contours of the wall-pressure fluctuations from DNS and two independently generated samples on the short training domain (LS x = 4πδ); (b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz) from an ensemble of 500 generated samples, compared with short-domain DNS.

Figure 6: Conditional reconstruction at Reτ = 180 on the long domain (LL x = 16πδ, Case 4): (a) Contours of the wall-pressure fluctuations from sparse sensor measurements (1% coverage), cubic interpolation, Patched FM reconstruction, and long-domain DNS ground truth; (b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz), comparing Patched FM, cubic interpolation, short-domain DNS, and long-domain DNS.

Figure 7: Unconditional generation at Reτ = 500 after hierarchical transfer from Reτ = 180: (a) Contours of the wallpressure fluctuations from DNS and two generated samples on the short training domain (LS x = 4πδ); (b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz) from 100 generated samples, compared with DNS.

Figure 8: Conditional reconstruction at Reτ = 500 on the long domain (LL x = 16πδ, Case 5): (a) Contours of the wall-pressure fluctuations from sparse sensor measurements (0.36% coverage), cubic interpolation, Patched FM reconstruction, and long-domain DNS; (b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz), comparing Patched FM, cubic interpolation, short-domain DNS, and long-domain DNS.

Figure 9: Unconditional generation at Reτ = 1000 after hierarchical transfer from Reτ = 500: (a) Contours of the wall-pressure fluctuations from DNS and two generated samples on the short training domain (LS x = 4πδ); (b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz) from 100 generated samples, compared with DNS.

Figure 10: Conditional reconstruction at Reτ = 1000 on the long domain (LL x = 16πδ, Case 6): (a) Contours of the wall-pressure fluctuations from sparse sensor measurements (0.25% coverage), cubic interpolation, Patched FM reconstruction, and long-domain DNS; (b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz), comparing Patched FM, cubic interpolation, short-domain DNS, and long-domain DNS.

Figure 11: Comparison of Patched FM against a baseline flow matching model trained on full-resolution data at Reτ = 180: (a,b) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra on the training domain (LS x = 4πδ); (c,d) streamwise and spanwise wavenumber spectra on the testing domain (LL x = 16πδ). The baseline is conditioned on the same sparse sensor measurements as Figure 6(a).

Figure 12: Comparison of inner-scaled versus outer-scaled Patched FM models at Reτ = 1000: (a,d) transfer-learned models (180 → 1000); (b,e) models trained from scratch; (c,f) training loss curves. Top row: inner-scaled coordinates; bottom row: outer-scaled coordinates.

Figure 13: Generation performance under severely limited training data (10 snapshots) at Reτ = 500: (a,d) streamwise and (b,e) spanwise power spectral densities for inner-scaled (top row) and outer-scaled (bottom row) transfer-learned models; (c,f) training loss curves comparing scratch training, standard transfer learning, and transfer learning with limited data. A loss descending below the generalization plateau (marginal-velocity solution) indicates progression toward memorization rather than improved generation [44].

Figure 14: Zero-shot conditional reconstruction at Reτ = 500 without any fine-tuning at the target Reynolds number: (a) Contours of the wall-pressure fluctuations from sparse sensor measurements (0.36% coverage), zero-shot reconstructions using models trained at Reτ = 180 and Reτ = 1000, and long-domain DNS ground truth (LL x = 16πδ, Case 5); (b) streamwise (top) and spanwise (bottom) wavenumber spectra Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz) comparing the two zero-shot reconstructions against long-domain DNS at Reτ = 500.

Figure 15: Computational cost of the Patched FM framework. (a) Cumulative training time (minutes, single NVIDIA L40 GPU) for three training strategies at Reτ = 1000: scratch training (120min), direct transfer learning (180 → 1000, 40min), and the proposed hierarchical transfer learning (180 → 500 → 1000, 15min), corresponding to 8× and 3× reductions relative to scratch training and direct transfer, respectively. (b) Per-sample wall-clock inference time for conditional generation at Reτ = 1000 (4800 × 800 grid) as a function of GPU count; single-GPU inference uses an NVIDIA A100 80 GB, multi-GPU inference uses NVIDIA 4090 24GB cards, and the 2×4090 configuration runs out of memory (OOM).

Figure A.16: Ablation study on the effect of sensor density on conditional generation quality at Reτ = 180: streamwise (left) and spanwise (right) wavenumber spectra of the wall-pressure fluctuations, Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz), for varying numbers of sensors. Results are compared against short- and long-domain DNS references.

Figure A.17: Sensitivity study on patch size at Reτ = 1000: streamwise (top) and spanwise (bottom) wavenumber spectra of the wall-pressure fluctuations, Φpp(kx) and Φpp(kz), for (a,d) a model trained with patch-size set Pb = {128, 56, 32} and evaluated at patch size 128; (b,e) a model trained with patch-size set Pb = {128, 56, 32} but evaluated at patch size 56; and (c,f) a model trained with patch-size set Ps = {56, 32, 16} and evaluated at patch size 56.
Table A.2: UNet architecture and training hyperparameters used in this work.
